stories.what: Yashasvi Jaiswal has been dropped from India's ODI squad for the England series despite scoring a century, in a major selection surprise that has set off debate about the selectors' priorities and team balance. Virat Kohli, meanwhile, has been named in the squad, marking his return to the Indian setup after a period away. The contrasting fortunes of the two batters — one in red-hot form yet left out, the other returning on reputation and past record — has made this one of the most talked-about selection calls in recent memory.
stories.lens: When a centurion gets dropped while a rested superstar walks back in, it tells everyday fans exactly how the selectors weigh current form against established stature — a debate that cuts to the heart of Indian cricket's selection culture.
stories.article:
India's selectors have dropped Yashasvi Jaiswal from the ODI squad to face England despite the young opener having scored a century — a decision that has left the cricketing world stunned and sparked an immediate, heated debate about the criteria being applied for national selection. Jaiswal, widely regarded as one of India's most exciting batting talents, was in demonstrable form yet found himself on the wrong side of a bewildering call.

The headline on the other side of the announcement is Virat Kohli's return to the Indian squad. Kohli, who had stepped back from certain formats and commitments in recent months, is back in contention for the England ODI series, a move that will delight his enormous fanbase and sharpen commercial and broadcast interest in the series considerably. His inclusion, however, inevitably raises questions about whether it came at Jaiswal's direct expense given the constraints of squad balance.

The selectorial logic remains opaque. Jaiswal's century would, in most cricketing cultures, be considered near-certain protection against the axe. The decision suggests the selectors are either thinking about a particular batting combination, Jaiswal's role in other formats, or a longer-term rotation plan — none of which has been publicly articulated. Critics have been quick to call it a missed opportunity to back consistent, in-form youth.

The England ODI series now carries an added subplot beyond the cricket itself: whether Kohli can justify his recall with runs, and whether Jaiswal's omission proves to be a temporary blip or a sign of deeper uncertainty about his ODI future. For Indian fans watching selection patterns closely, this is precisely the kind of call that defines how the team's transition era is being managed.
key_facts: Yashasvi Jaiswal dropped from India's England ODI squad despite scoring a century
Virat Kohli named in the squad, marking his return to the Indian setup
Selection decision widely debated given Jaiswal's recent form
India face England in upcoming ODI series

stories.headline: Did Amitabh Bachchan shelve Khabardar to avoid being overshadowed by Kamal Haasan?
stories.what: A long-circulating piece of Bollywood lore has resurfaced: that Amitabh Bachchan pulled out of — or effectively shelved — the film Khabardar because he feared being outshone by co-star Kamal Haasan. The Indian Express revisits the story behind the ill-fated collaboration between two of Hindi cinema's biggest names, a project that was never completed. The episode, if true, would represent a rare instance of Bollywood's biggest superstar stepping back from a project for fear of artistic competition.
stories.lens: The Bachchan-Haasan rivalry anecdote is a window into the ego politics and star-power calculations that have always shaped — and sometimes killed — ambitious Bollywood projects.
stories.article:
Decades after the fact, one of Bollywood's most tantalising 'what-ifs' is being revisited: did Amitabh Bachchan effectively kill the film Khabardar because he feared being outperformed by Kamal Haasan? The Indian Express digs into the rumour that has persisted in film circles for years, examining the circumstances that led to the project being shelved.

The story, as it has circulated in Bollywood lore, goes that Bachchan — then at the peak of his 'angry young man' superstardom — grew uneasy about sharing screen space with Kamal Haasan, widely regarded as one of the most technically gifted actors of his generation. Haasan's ability to transform physically and emotionally for roles was already becoming legendary, and the concern, reportedly, was that audiences might walk away talking more about him than the top-billed Bachchan.

Such dynamics were not uncommon in the Hindi film industry of that era. The star system placed enormous importance on hierarchy — who got more screen time, whose name appeared first, and crucially, who audiences went home remembering. A multi-starrer that upset that calculus could be commercially and reputationally damaging for the bigger name. Whether Bachchan's team made a deliberate call or circumstances conspired is something the report examines without definitive conclusion.

What makes the anecdote enduring is what it says about two parallel legends: Bachchan, the undisputed commercial king of Hindi cinema, and Haasan, the actor's actor who rarely played by Bollywood's star-system rules. A completed Khabardar would have been one of the most fascinating screen pairings of that era — and its absence remains one of Indian cinema's great unmade films.
key_facts: Film Khabardar was reportedly shelved after Amitabh Bachchan's exit
Kamal Haasan was co-star; Bachchan allegedly feared being overshadowed
Project represents one of Bollywood's most notable 'unmade films'

stories.headline: How Om Puri's craft silenced Raaj Kumar's on-set insults
stories.what: A behind-the-scenes anecdote about the late actors Om Puri and Raaj Kumar has resurfaced, describing how Puri's raw acting ability effectively stopped Raaj Kumar — notoriously imperious on film sets — from directing his trademark contempt at him. The story, revisited by The Indian Express, illustrates the complex hierarchies and personality clashes that defined Hindi cinema's golden era. It also serves as a tribute to Om Puri's formidable reputation as one of the most technically accomplished actors of his generation.
stories.lens: For fans of classic Hindi cinema, this anecdote captures something real about how genuine craft — not stardom — was the ultimate currency even in an industry driven by ego and hierarchy.
stories.article:
On the sets of Hindi cinema's golden era, few personalities were as intimidating as Raaj Kumar — an actor famed as much for his imperious manner and sharp tongue as for his screen presence. A story now doing the rounds, revisited by The Indian Express, describes how the late Om Puri managed to do what few could: render Raaj Kumar's disdain utterly ineffective, simply through the power of his acting.

The anecdote captures a moment where Raaj Kumar, known for belittling co-stars and crew, found himself unable to sustain his usual condescension in the face of Puri's extraordinary craft. Puri — trained at the Film and Television Institute of India (FTII) and the National School of Drama — brought an intensity to his performances that was virtually impossible to dismiss or diminish, even for someone of Raaj Kumar's formidable ego.

Om Puri's career was built on exactly this kind of quiet authority. From Ardh Satya to Jaane Bhi Do Yaaron, he repeatedly demonstrated that pure acting technique could command a room and a camera in ways that star power alone could not. The fact that even Raaj Kumar — no shrinking violet himself — had to concede that space is a telling measure of Puri's stature.

Both actors are now gone, but stories like these keep their legacies alive in vivid, human terms. The anecdote is less about rivalry and more about the unspoken grammar of respect on a film set — that ultimately, craft speaks loudest.
key_facts: Om Puri trained at FTII and National School of Drama
Raaj Kumar was known for his imperious, often dismissive manner on sets
Both actors are now deceased; anecdote revisits Hindi cinema's golden era

stories.headline: After publicly demanding Samay Raina be 'paraded on a donkey', Shaktimaan actor Mukesh Khanna ends up collaborating with him
stories.what: Mukesh Khanna, the actor famed for playing Shaktimaan, had previously made headlines by saying stand-up comedian and roast-king Samay Raina 'should be paraded on a donkey' — a sharp public rebuke. Now, in a striking reversal, Khanna has gone ahead and worked with Raina on a project. The about-turn has drawn wide attention online, given how emphatic Khanna's original criticism was. It underscores the peculiar pull of Raina's growing cultural platform.
stories.lens: When one of Bollywood's most vocal moral guardians ends up on the same set as the man he publicly shamed, it says something sharp about how creator-economy clout now reshapes old-guard celebrity calculations.
stories.article:
Mukesh Khanna, the actor who became a household name as the caped superhero Shaktimaan, once called for Samay Raina to be publicly humiliated — specifically, that the stand-up comedian and roast organiser 'should be paraded on a donkey'. The statement was made in the context of Raina's roast culture, which Khanna viewed as disrespectful and morally corrosive. It was the kind of condemnation that, in an earlier era, would have ended any prospect of collaboration.

That makes what happened next all the more striking. Khanna has now worked with Raina on a project, a development reported by The Indian Express that has set social media buzzing with a mixture of amusement and irony. The details of the collaboration have not been fully disclosed, but the mere fact of it is the story — a loudly declared enemy becoming a professional associate.

The episode fits a familiar pattern in Indian entertainment, where public feuds between legacy Bollywood figures and new-age digital creators often thaw quietly when audiences — and opportunity — demand it. Raina, who rose to national prominence through his roast shows and his tenure on India's Got Latent, commands a young, massive fanbase that legacy stars increasingly cannot afford to ignore.

For Khanna, long known as a keeper of traditional values in entertainment, the collaboration will inevitably invite questions about principle versus pragmatism. For Raina, it is another trophy of cultural crossover — proof that even his harshest critics eventually find their way to his door.
key_facts: Mukesh Khanna had said Samay Raina 'should be paraded on a donkey'
Khanna has now worked with Raina despite the public criticism
Raina is a prominent stand-up comedian known for roast shows

stories.headline: Sona Mohapatra says Bollywood heartbreak songs are 'reserved for men', questions why women's pain goes unsung
stories.what: Singer Sona Mohapatra has called out a longstanding gender imbalance in Bollywood music, arguing that heartbreak songs are overwhelmingly written for and performed by men, leaving women's emotional experiences largely invisible in mainstream Hindi film music. She pointedly asked 'is man making love to himself?' — highlighting the absurdity of a musical landscape where male longing is central but a female counterpart is absent. The comment has reignited debate about representation in the Hindi film music industry.
stories.lens: Bollywood produces hundreds of songs a year that shape how millions of Indians process love and loss — if women's heartbreak is systematically absent from that canon, it reflects and reinforces a real cultural blind spot.
stories.article:
Singer and activist Sona Mohapatra has fired a pointed critique at Bollywood's songwriting establishment, arguing that the genre of heartbreak — one of Hindi film music's most lucrative and beloved — is effectively 'reserved for men'. Speaking publicly, Mohapatra questioned why the emotional vocabulary of romantic loss in mainstream Indian cinema is almost entirely constructed around the male perspective, with women either absent or peripheral.

Her sharpest line was a rhetorical question directed at the absurdity of the situation: 'Is man making love to himself?' The quip cuts to the heart of her argument — that if Bollywood endlessly produces songs about men pining, grieving, and longing, but rarely gives women the same emotional real estate, it implies a one-sided universe where female desire and heartbreak simply do not exist.

Mohapatra is no stranger to controversy within the industry. She has previously spoken out on issues ranging from music royalties to the treatment of independent artists, making her a consistent dissenting voice in an industry not known for welcoming internal criticism. Her remarks, reported by The Times of India, have drawn both agreement and pushback on social media, with many users citing specific songs that prove her point and others pointing to exceptions.

The debate touches on something structural: Bollywood's songwriting and composing fraternity has historically been male-dominated, which critics argue shapes whose emotional experiences get centred in the music that ultimately forms the soundtrack to Indian life.
key_facts: Sona Mohapatra says Bollywood heartbreak songs are written primarily for men
She asked 'is man making love to himself?' to highlight the gender gap
The comment reignites debate about gender representation in Hindi film music

stories.headline: Indian kids' IP brand behind 'Emomee' launches its first animated series
stories.what: The Indian children's intellectual property brand behind 'Emomee' has rolled out its first full animated series, marking a significant step for homegrown kids' content in a market long dominated by foreign IP. The launch, reported exclusively by Variety, signals growing confidence among Indian creators in building original characters and franchises aimed at young domestic audiences. Emomee is positioned as a character-driven brand built around emotional literacy for children, making the animated series a natural extension of the IP.
stories.lens: As global streaming platforms flood Indian children's screens with foreign content, an Indian-origin kids' IP graduating to a full animated series is a meaningful step toward building homegrown franchises that reflect Indian children's lives and emotions.
stories.article:
Emomee, an Indian children's intellectual property brand, has launched its first animated series — a milestone for homegrown kids' entertainment that has drawn attention from the international trade press. Variety, which broke the story exclusively, reported that the brand behind Emomee is now moving beyond its origins to deliver a full serialised animated format aimed at young Indian audiences.

Emomee is built around the concept of emotional literacy — the characters and narratives are designed to help children identify, understand, and express their feelings. This positions it in a growing global niche of purpose-driven children's content, one that has seen significant success internationally with properties like Inside Out. The animated series format allows the IP to deepen character development and reach children through the medium they consume most naturally.

The Indian kids' content market is substantial but has historically been dominated by international franchises — from Disney and Nickelodeon to Japanese anime — leaving limited shelf space for original Indian IP to establish itself. Brands like Chhota Bheem have shown that homegrown characters can achieve massive scale, and Emomee's move into animation is a bid to follow that path with a more emotionally nuanced proposition.

The Variety exclusive suggests the brand is also positioning itself for international attention, not just domestic growth. For Indian animation studios and IP creators, a trade-press spotlight of this kind can open doors to co-production deals, international licensing, and streaming platform partnerships that were once out of reach for Indian children's content.
key_facts: Emomee is an Indian kids' IP brand focused on emotional literacy for children
The brand has launched its first full animated series
The launch was reported exclusively by Variety, signalling international industry interest
